speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape 4 of the interview with George Ginsburg on the 4th of September, 1996. You were talking about getting extra sausages to sustain your food supply. Did you-- were you able to do that for very long?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Those sausages is as good as gold. You see, when you-- you divide it quick, you change it into other things, Like vodka, bread, butter, main things. Good soap, things like that you can buy for that. Even a slice like that buys it for you, in different-- in a different direction. That means you can do business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I did to survive. And then I'm able to give my ration to somebody I like or a friend of mine. My soup I never, you know, hardly ate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did this go on for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a few times, not any particular time. But a few times it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you working--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went-- I had to go out, because it became too suspicious. And I went into-- and I went to the Union, what you call, factory, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which were making-- ammunition factory of the camp. There were thousands of people working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- can you remember what year this was or what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month? Can you remember? What time of the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there for-- for eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ammunition factory. And did it go on into 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, tell me about the work there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a kapo called Schultz. oberkapo. He was a Jew, a Yugoslav or Croatian Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very bad. If you were on the right side-- but he was schizophrenia. You know, he could be good and then-- most of the people in the camp were schizophrenia anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] including the high SS were schizophrenic, because they adopted Jewish children from the-- from the-- they didn't let them go to the gas chambers. They used them as pets, cats and dogs, like playing with their children. When they got tired of them, they put them in the gas chambers and got new ones. They played in their homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes, I did, because a couple of times I was cleaning-- I was selected to clean or deliver laundry in the later stage to them in their homes, like a laundryman, so I saw that. I saw the Jewish children playing there, beautiful dressed and all. And then I went to the Union. We worked in ammunition factory together with women. We had connection with women. So a lot of men wanted to have that connection with women and they paid me for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paid you for?

speaker: interviewee
text: For delivering letters and notes and finding out which women are with me, or-- some women which are working there are working with their wives in-- in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they pay you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to pay me in bread or in rations or in cigarettes, mostly cigarettes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mostly I took cigarettes. They could-- they could change it themself into cigarettes and pay me in cigarettes. Cigarettes was a currency.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do that a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I risked my life. I risked something. If they find notes for me from somebody else, I get 10 and 15 on-- on my back. Nothing is without risks here. And why should I do it for strangers?

speaker: interviewer
text: How were people treated in that factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the factory itself, pretty good. They were-- because the SS who were [INAUDIBLE] were specialists like engineers and things like that. They put them in there. They were in the SS uniform, but they were engineers and turner and fitters and things like that. They were professional people. They were not animals from the bush, you know. So they treated the prisoners like a-- like a professional, like their apprentice or something. And then there were overseers, which were not professionals. They were in charge of the whole group of prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they treat the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Screaming, shouting, pushing, usual thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any-- anything besides screaming and shouting, anything more than that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, hitting, yeah, sometimes. But it was in general much better than the outside, much better.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Describe the work you did there.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the machine I was on a lathe, turning, turning and fitting on a lathe. And then the women were packing. And it was really factory work, you know. And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they making in that factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in that particular time [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there were three women-- that was already towards the end of '44-- three women who were arrested. They were delivering dynamite to the camp, to the men. And in that particular time, Crematorium 4 was blown up. The SS was shot. The scharfuhrer who was in charge of Crematorium 4 was thrown in the furnace by the Sonderkommando. Sonderkommando people which are working in the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish prisoners. They get whatever they want. They can drink. They can eat. They're all strong. They changed them every six months. They killed them and then put new ones in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they knew that, the people working in that job knew-- so tell me-- tell me what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And in end of '44, we saw a big fire in Auschwitz [INAUDIBLE] lager, on the horizon, big fire, you know, like red. The sky was red. And the Crematorium 4 was burning.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was Crematoria 4?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Zygmund told me. The fire brigade went out from our camp. The whole fire brigade with fire trucks and everything went out there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know that the-- the German had been killed and thrown into the--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Zygmund, from the fire brigades.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did he know?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were 500 people escaping, Sonderkommando. 480 people escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were they, Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews. Sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they escape too?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they ran off.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But they got caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of--

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them, unfortunately, with dogs and everything. They couldn't go far.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were sending out thousands of troops after them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about how these-- three or four women, did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there were three or four. I don't know exactly. They were hanged in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: They hung them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had they done? What had they done exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: They provided the ammunition from Union, where I was working. They provided to blow up the-- blow up the furnaces and blow up the tower.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were working in the same place as you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were-- they were shifting explosives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they smuggled some of those explosives--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, and supplied it to the man. And the man smuggled it to Sonderkommado. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's how the whole furnaces were blown up, and that the watchtowers were blown up. They put ammunition under the watchtower with machine guns and it flew up in the air.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they hanged the women, did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were hanged in Birkenau. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were hanged in Birkenau. They were-- they were in Block-- they were in Block 11. They were all in Block 11, but they were transported to Birkenau to be hung. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Slovakians, they were, three young women. Unfortunately, there's no memorial at the moment for them at all saying that they were Jewish women. Anyhow, I worked in Union for quite a while. And then I got-- there was not enough-- there was enough to-- business to do, you know. It was a factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got the hint from somebody there was a vacation, through my connections which I made in the meantime. I was already older prisoner. I was already older survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got into a SS laundry. That was the best job I ever had. It was under the roof. It was warm. It was clean. My uniform was clean. Everything was picked. And I could-- I had the freedom of moving around. Delivering the law only for this obersturmfuhrer, this unterscharfuhrer, this, this, this, and everybody was giving me a few cigarettes and this, and good done and well done, can you do my shirt, et cetera, my pants. And I was-- there were about only 10 people working there. The whole kommando was 10 people. And I was amongst the 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they also Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all, no. Four Jews. The rest was not Jews. The foreman was not Jews. There was no beating. There was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in the camp generally? Did you see any beatings?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I saw beatings every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, describe-- describe one of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw beatings every day and I saw hangings everyday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hangings of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they hanging?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them were Serb partisans, Serbs, with the-- you know? And some of them were people who tried to escape. I was-- they put a board around them. I-- I-- I was trying to escape. And they hang them up. And some of them were-- that means local prisoners, Poles mostly, not Jews, Poles. And some of them were Serb partisans, they caught them. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some of them were punished prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they hang Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They killed them. They didn't have to hang them. They killed them in the camp. They didn't have to string them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe some-- you said you saw a lot of brutality. Describe things you saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, some of the people they hanged-- there was a fellow, as I said to you, a [INAUDIBLE] called Tadek. Tadek was a Polnish, fellow, very good looking, tall. He was in love with a Jewish girl, a Slovakian girl, in Birkenau. He fell in lover with here. They had something together. He stole a-- he had free access to go anywhere. He stole a [INAUDIBLE]. He stole a uniform. He took her and he drove away, to Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: They caught him and her. Both got hung in front of everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw him hanged?

speaker: interviewee
text: End of '43. Yeah. We all saw it. The hanging was taking place in the appell in the morning. Was prepared a table and on that table was chairs. And they stood on the chairs with a noose around us the thing-- around the structure. And then the chair-- the chairs were kicked away by the SS. And they floated. They dangled. That was it. No-- no-- no-- no hood. No nothing. And the most courageous people I have ever seen hung was the Serbs. They spit in the Germans' faces and said, we'll get you. You won't survive that war. We'll get you. They stood up. And as a matter of fact, I heard from other people-- I don't care what they say here now about the Serbs. I got great respect for the Serbs, because the Serbs were the only one who were interfering with the transports. They stopped two or three transports. They killed all the Germans on top of the guards and they opened the doors and they took the Jews with them in the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: From people who had been taken in the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you meet these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in displaced persons camps in Germany [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, describe-- we'll go back to your description of some of the other brutalities that you saw, that you were witness to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, anybody who was in Auschwitz and he-- he had plenty-- he saw further than just his barrack would witness brutalities all the time, particular by-- by-- by Germans, by German kapos, by oberkapos, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] by Polnish-- half-Polnish and half-Germans, which are from Schleswig, you know, Poznania.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kick them, mistreat them, beat them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know why they'd do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were sadists and the-- and those people were helpless and weak. And they wanted to make a good eye in the Germans' eyes. They want to show themself.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have seen even people where Polnish-- Polnish kapos kick the-- kick the prisoners and the-- and the Germans said, what do you do that for? Stop it. We need that man to work. We don't need him dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans had to stop prisoner kapos to-- there was more people killed by prisoners than there were by Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: By prisoners, you mean what sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Inmates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. But what-- what nationality were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Croatians, Germans, Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any. None of them were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You see, in Auschwitz, we hardly had any seniority in Jewish-- all the Jewish kapos and things like that was all happening in Birkenau [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or in the Gypsy camps. But in-- in Auschwitz, there was no-- no seniority of Jews at all. They were all that nationality, or German criminals which were in charge, like oberkapos, green-- green-- you see, there was a difference between green triangle and red triangle. The red triangle were political, where the green triangle were criminals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about-- give us a typical day when you were in the laundry. What time would you have to wake up? How were you woken up? Where were you sleeping? What kind of conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sleeping in a very good block, with people which had already good kommandos. We call them kommandos, working kommandos. We all had our little wardrobe even, our little cupboards, where we can lock in our food and our personal belongings. We didn't have to keep it under the mattress, like in other barracks. And we went to work at-- after the-- after the roll call at 6 o'clock, we went to work. Went to work. We had a-- a [INAUDIBLE] which is-- which is a role who irons the clothing. We had washing troughs. We boiled the laundry in copper-- in copper-- big copper kettles with wooden sticks. We had soap. We had everything in there, at work. We had drying cupboards and drying rooms where we're drying the-- the laundry. And we are working exclusively for the SS administration and SS in charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have to take-- did you take laundry to the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Not by myself. I always had somebody to deliver and I had a guard behind me, outside every time you go outside the gates, you had to report and things were written, time-- the time and who passes and what number passes and where you're going. And then when you come in, you had to be in at a certain time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see as you went around the camp delivering laundry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the SS barracks, they were outside the camp. That's the barracks, outside the camp. If you go further in the camp, we call it the [NON-ENGLISH] was 50 kilometers. There was a second-- a second security guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. But not delivering laundry. That's when I went first, when-- with the first kommandos we went outside the 50 kilometers. And we saw civilian workers. We saw civilian Polnish workers working in the fields. And they were not far. Some of the kommandos, they worked together with civilian Polnish workers, but all the time under guards and dogs, naturally. And and then-- and then after-- after 50 kilometers, you're more or less outside, you know. And I delivered the laundry and things like that to the SS houses, which were like a small house. And the staff, they had their staff there, their woman whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any particular friends at the camp? You talked about a few friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other friends at the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what did you do--

speaker: interviewee
text: I also had SS guards which I deal with on the black market.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you deal with them on the black market?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- they-- how I deal with them. I gave them cigarettes. They gave me vodka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get the cigarettes from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from my connections. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the connections?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told you, I-- about the things I did and risked my life on the line. And once I got caught and I got punished. They hung me up and they beat 10 on my back with a-- with a oxtail.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they hang you up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, on cups and hang me up on chains on the ceiling and they swung me. And there was one fellow standing there, oberkapo, and he hit me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I know the fellow who stood by and counting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Unterscharfuhrer Moll. He had a pig farm once, a long time ago. Moll, he was known. He said, if you don't know me, you'll know me. That was his speech.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you do outside of the times you had to work? How did you-- how did people occupy themselves? How did you occupy yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to different blocks to talk to people. I made my connections.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. You can-- you can go. You can go anywhere you like.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was freedom of walking within the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, until a certain time.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Til what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 10 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you visited people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did people do when they visited, apart from talking? Was there ever any entertainment of any kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Entertainment. No. There was no entertainment. A bit of singing, a bit of this, a bit of that, that's about it. There was not much entertainment. The only entertainment that the-- the SS got, you see. If they get a boxer like Saunders and they put another one against him and they were all sitting around. And they let them thresh it out and they were-- that's the way they were entertaining themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Saunders?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a friend of mine. He was in the [INAUDIBLE]. He was champion of Holland, a boxer. That's the one who survived. He's in Curacao.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was-- he would be used by the Nazis as part of their entertainment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Boxing. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he win?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They put even SS against him. He beat them up in the ring.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very-- they laughed. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wasn't punished for beating the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You're asking me about atrocities. I saw atrocities. I saw Orthodox Jews. They put them into thick coats, into ladies' fur coats. They put rope around them and they put them thick Russian gloves on and Russian caps on. And they put Dobermans on them and they torn them apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know when. '42, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was more--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were laughing. That was the SS entertainment.

speaker: interviewer
text: More than once you saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any other work you ever did at the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was doing [NON-ENGLISH]. That means cleaning the barracks. I was doing that. And sometime-- once I was helping in the-- in the hospitals. I was doing that, helping a bit and carrying a bit and doing things. And other work I did in the camp, I was also in the [NON-ENGLISH], I remember now. That means in the-- it's called-- you know, to help the waterworks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, dig-- dig-- dig channels and put pipes in. We did that too. And once I had a bit of bad luck. I struck one of those [NON-ENGLISH] and he was completely drunk. he got hold of us, two guards. That was before, in the beginning. And I started to dig. He said, make a hole. We dug a hole and there were four of us. And we dug hole. But-- and then I realized what I was doing. I was digging my own grave. And he was so drunk. And he said he wanted to shoot us in the grave and said, a German bullet is too good for you [INAUDIBLE] Jews. And he put it back and walked away. A German bullet is not-- is too good for you. The four of us stood there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Did-- did you know who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. And the-- and the-- and the infamous one I met was a fellow called Pohl, P-O-H-L. He was in charge, second to Hitler, administration and economics of the SS. He ran the Politische Abteilung in Auschwitz. He was often there. He was tried in the Nuremberg trial and he was hung.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the connection? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he came to the SS laundry and I delivered-- I delivered to his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he like? How did he treat you? Oh, no. High officers don't touch you. They don't do the dirty work. They don't do dirty work. The little-- they let the little ones to the dirty work. And was there any other work you did at the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] I was sorting-- sorting from people who were gassed-- I was sorting their belongings, their clothes and their things like that. And we were disinfecting it. The Zyklon B was thrown in-- and the same gas they killed the-- killed the people with. And after about 15, 20 minutes, they put the fans on and we were going in with gas masks to remove the clothes. The clothes were sorted.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they sorted?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, blouse by blouse and suit by suit and this. And they were sent to Germany, to the Winterhilfe. You know what the Winterhilfe is? To help poor people in Germany or bombed-out people with clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what period of time was this that you were working in this job?

speaker: interviewee
text: There also-- also in about I think it was also '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was before your job in the laundry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, yeah. Everything was before the laundry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the laundry job your last job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, what happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: The glasses were sorted, the eye-- the thing. And then, you know-- and they came to supervise it and was counted and registered. Oh, the Germans are very efficient with registering and counting and things like that, very efficient, like computers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew exactly how many they have of each. And they were recording everything, because after the war they found everything recorded. And that was it. And what else can I tell you? Well, tell me what happened-- when did things start to change at Auschwitz? When did you know that the war wasn't going so well for the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got the news about Stalingrad from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: They talked.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Germans talked. Not the Germans talked. Those idiots they hired them. They were Ukrainians. They were Ukrainians who used to be in the Russian army and the Soviet army. And they-- there-- there was a general called Vlasov. And he took all the men out of the prisoner camps, all the Ukrainians and things like that. And he formed a SS battalion, which was authorized by the German government. And they were guards in the camp. And the Croatians were guards in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Estonians were guards in the camp, and the Lithuanians and Latvians were guards in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who did you hear from that--

speaker: interviewee
text: From them. From them. We-- we all knew about that. There was a underground in Auschwitz. There was a underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can only tell you what I know, because each cell didn't know from another cell.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, tell--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was organized in such a way that if they torture somebody, he only can give away two people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was affiliated.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, describe what your involvement--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the people-- to the people who were in there. It's to gather information, pass them on very slowly to the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the purpose. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very carefully [? to whom. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what role did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, just-- just carrying information. Nothing active. Nothing with ammunition. Nothing with escaping. I was not in escape committee. The escape committee was organized by Poles, by sophisticated Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. Now you see, in-- even if a Jew escapes from Auschwitz, he would not get far, because of the anti-Semitic atmosphere outside the camp. They would have been given away.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll finish the tape there and go on to the next one.
